draft.introduction: Romans 3:21–31 presents Paul’s doctrine of justification apart from the law, through faith in Jesus Christ. This passage is the theological heart of the epistle and the foundation of Reformation soteriology.

translation.text: But now the righteousness of God has been manifested apart from the law, although the Law and the Prophets bear witness to it—the righteousness of God through faith in Jesus Christ for all who believe. For there is no distinction: for all have sinned and fall short of the glory of God, and are justified by his grace as a gift, through the redemption that is in Christ Jesus.
translation.original_text: νυνὶ δὲ χωρὶς νόμου δικαιοσύνη θεοῦ πεφανέρωται

translator_notes.note: The phrase “fall short of the glory of God” (ὑστεροῦνται τῆς δόξης τοῦ θεοῦ) has been read by Eastern Orthodox theologians as implying that humans were created for participation in divine glory—theosis—and that sin is precisely the failure to achieve this destiny.
